pages.title: Introduction
pages.description: Own Auth is an auth library that runs in your backend and stores authentication data in Postgres or Cloudflare D1. Users, sessions, magic links, phone codes, API keys, and organisations stay in tables you control.

pages.title: Quickstart
pages.description: Get your first login working in under five minutes. You need Node.js 20+ and a Postgres database.

pages.title: Installation
pages.description: Install Own Auth, configure Postgres, and create the required database tables.

pages.title: Configuration
pages.description: Create an auth.ts file in your project. This is the single entry point for all auth operations.

pages.title: Passwords
pages.description: Email and password authentication. The most common auth method. Users sign up with an email and a password, and sign in with the same credentials.

pages.title: Magic Links
pages.description: Passwordless sign-in via email. The user enters their email, receives a link, clicks it, and they are in. No password to remember, no password to steal.

pages.title: Phone Login
pages.description: Sign in with a phone number and a one-time SMS code. The user enters their phone number, receives a text with a 6-digit code, types it in, and they are signed in.

pages.title: Email Verification
pages.description: Confirm that a user owns the email address they signed up with. After sign-up, send a verification email. The user opens the link and their email is marked as verified.

pages.title: Password Reset
pages.description: Let users set a new password when they have forgotten their current one. The user enters their email, receives a reset link, opens it, and enters a new password.

pages.title: OAuth And External Providers
pages.description: Use Google, GitHub, or Apple redirect OAuth, Google One Tap, or a trusted verified identity from a native provider SDK.

pages.title: Multi-Factor Authentication
pages.description: Add TOTP authenticator apps, one-time recovery codes, and pending MFA challenges without creating a session after only one factor.

pages.title: Passkeys
pages.description: Use WebAuthn passkeys for primary sign-in or as a user-verified second factor.

pages.title: Session Management
pages.description: Sessions are how users stay signed in between requests. When a user signs in, Own Auth creates a session and returns a token. That token is sent with each later request to identify the user.

pages.title: Organisations
pages.description: Organisations are teams, workspaces, or tenants. A user can belong to multiple organisations. Each organisation has members with roles. Built in from the start, not something you bolt on later.

pages.title: Members
pages.description: Members are users who belong to an organisation. Each member has a role that determines what they can do within that organisation.

pages.title: Roles
pages.description: Use built-in organisation roles and permission checks.

pages.title: Invites
pages.description: Invite users to join an organisation by email. The invited person receives an email containing a single-use link and accepts the organisation membership through the application.

pages.title: API Keys
pages.description: Let users of an application create API keys to access that application's API programmatically. Keys are shown once at creation, stored hashed, scoped, and revocable at any time.

pages.title: Rate Limiting
pages.description: Own Auth rate-limits sensitive authentication operations automatically. Postgres and Cloudflare D1 store counters beside the auth data, with no separate rate-limit service to configure.

pages.title: Audit Logs
pages.description: Audit logs record who performed an authentication action, who or what it affected, when it happened, and the available request context. Own Auth writes these events automatically as part of each supported operation.

pages.title: Security Model
pages.description: How Own Auth protects an authentication system. This page covers the security decisions built into the library, including what is hashed, what expires, what is rate-limited, and what remains the application's responsibility.

pages.title: HTTP Handler
pages.description: Expose Own Auth through one framework-neutral Web Request and Response handler with secure cookies, CSRF checks, typed errors, and generated OpenAPI.

pages.title: TypeScript Client
pages.description: Call the Own Auth HTTP handler from browser TypeScript or React with typed methods and shared current-session state.

pages.title: Plugins
pages.description: Extend Own Auth through namespaced methods, endpoints, hooks, rate limits, migrations, clients, and OpenAPI contracts.

pages.title: Cloudflare D1
pages.description: Run Own Auth in Cloudflare Workers with durable D1 storage and rate limiting.

pages.title: Observability
pages.description: Collect privacy-safe OpenTelemetry traces and metrics for Own Auth operations, HTTP handlers, provider calls, delivery, plugins, and rate-limit denials.

pages.title: Auth API
pages.description: Methods exposed by an Own Auth instance.

pages.title: Next.js
pages.description: Use Own Auth from a Next.js App Router route handler.

pages.title: Express
pages.description: Use Own Auth from an Express route handler.

pages.title: Hono
pages.description: Use Own Auth from a Hono route handler.

pages.title: Fastify
pages.description: Use Own Auth from a Fastify route handler.
